subject: Computer Networking
title: HTTPS and TLS
description: A quick win: understand HTTPS and TLS.

hook.text: HTTP sends everything in plain text. Passwords, card numbers, messages — anyone could read them.

buildup.text: TLS wraps HTTP in encryption. The combination is called HTTPS — the 'S' stands for Secure.

discovery.text: TLS starts with a handshake: the server proves its identity with a certificate signed by a trusted authority.

twist.text: After the handshake, both sides derive a shared secret key. All data flows through that encrypted channel.

climax.text: Modern browsers mark plain HTTP sites as 'Not Secure.' HTTPS is now the baseline, not the exception.

punchline.text: TLS makes HTTP private. That's why the padlock matters.

quiz.question: How does a server prove its identity in TLS?
options: By presenting a certificate signed by a trusted authority
By sending its source code to the client
By sharing its private encryption key
